titleStmt.title: Remarks on A Bold Stroke for a Wife
The British Theatre

analytic.title: Remarks [on A Bold Stroke for a Wife]

monogr.title: A Bold Stroke for a Wife;
A Comedy, in Five Acts; By Mrs. Centlivre. As Performed at the Theatre Royal, Drury Lane. Printed under the Authority of the Managers from the Prompt Book. With Remarks by Mrs. Inchbald.

quotation.p: All quotation marks and apostrophes have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
Quotation marks that indicate block quotes have been silently eliminated.

hyphenation.p: Any dashes occurring in line breaks have been removed.
All hyphens have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
em-dashes have been recorded originally using the —

normalization.p: Page numbers appear at the beginning of each page, no matter where originally placed.
Compositors' catchwords and running titles have been silently eliminated.
& has been used for the ampersand sign.
All other characters, those with accents, non-breaking spaces, etc., have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
Writing in other hands appearing on these manuscripts has been indicated as such, the content recorded in brackets.

category.catDesc: author of main text and of works reviewed, if relevant, using LOC authority name headings.

list.item: Corrected titles in teiHeader to remove all items listed as NOT to be used in the Encoding Decisions, Header section, which I also updated to make more clear.
Added myself (with xml:id) as technical editor to the titleStmt

head.bibl:
Elizabeth Inchbald
REMARKS [on A Bold Stroke for a Wife]."Remarks." A Bold Stroke for a Wife; A Comedy, In Five Acts; By Mrs. Centlivre. As Performed at the Theatre Royal, Drury Lane. Printed Under the Authority of the Managers From the Prompt Book. With Remarks by Mrs. Inchbald. London: Printed for Longman, Hurst, Rees, and Orme, Paternoster Row, pp. 3-5. The British Theatre; or, A Collection of Plays, Which Are Acted At the Theatres Royal, Drury Lane, Covent Garden, and Haymarket. Printed Under the Authority of the Managers from the Prompt Books. With Biographical and Critical Remarks, by Mrs. Inchbald. In Twenty-Five Volumes. Vol. XI. Busy Body. Wonder. Bold Stroke for a Wife. George Barnwell. Fatal Curiosity. London: Printed for Longman, Hurst, Rees, and Orme, Paternoster Row. 1808. The first performance of this play was staged at the Lincoln's Inn Fields Theatre on February 3rd, 1718. Laura DeWitt and Mary A. Waters edited this essay for The Criticism Archive.
______

div.p: Susannah Centlivre, the writer of this play, says of it, in her Dedication to the Duke of Wharton:— All that I have to assert in favour of this piece is, that the plot is entirely new, and the incidents wholly owing to my own invention; not borrowed from our own, or translated from the works of any foreign poet; so that they have at least the charm of novelty to recommend them.From the dedication "To His Grace Philip, Duke and Marquis of Wharton, &c." preceding the 1718 publication of Centlivre's A Bold Stroke for a Wife (p. iv).

It would at present be more honourable to the authoress, that a reader should believe she had inconsiderately adopted the scenes of another, in the following play, than invented them herself. Still, in that supposition, much blame would attach to her taste and morality for the choice she had made in the adoption.Despite its popularity with audiences, this play was widely met with objection from critics on moral grounds. The plot revolves around Colonel Fainwell's attempts to win the approval of the four guardians of Anne Lovely, an heiress whom he wishes to marry. Anne Lovely's father appointed her four guardians (all of whom represent stock characters) with greatly disparate tastes and interests to ensure no suitor could win the approval of all four, hence preventing his daughter from marrying. Throughout the play, Fainwell employs numerous disguises and methods of deceit to win the approval of all four guardians. The satire of the play conveyed Centlivre's support of Whig party, denouncing capitalism, religious hypocrisy, and the Tory party.

It is deeply to be lamented, that, at the time the most ingenious and witty of the English dramatists lived, there was no restraint, as at this period, upon the immorality of the stage. Plays would have come down to the present age, under such restrictions, less brilliant in humour and repartee, with fewer eulogiums from the admirers of wit; but with fewer reproaches from the wise and the good, upon the evil tendency of the dramatic art.

The happy effect of the moral dramas of this æra, in impressing those persons with just sentiments who attend no other place of instruction but a theatre, has not yet erased from the mind of the prejudiced former ill consequences, from former plays. b 2

Mrs. Centlivre, as a woman, falls more particularly under censure than her cotemporary writers:—though her temptations, to please the degraded taste of the public, were certainly more vehement than those of the authors, who wrote at that time; for they were men whose fortunes were not wholly dependent on their mental exertions; yet, the virtue of fortitude is expected from a female, when delicacy is the object which tries it; and the authoress of this comedy should have laid down her pen, and taken, in exchange, the meanest implement of labour, rather than have imitated the licentious example given her by the renowned poets of those days.

That Mrs. Centlivre was unfortunate from her birth, an orphan in her tender years, and a friendless wanderer at that age when most she required protection,Although there is scant evidence surrounding Centlivre's early life, it is generally believed that her father died when she was three years old, her mother died shortly after remarrying, and her stepfather married soon after that, though the timeline is unclear. Centlivre's early biographers posed two conflicting theories of her life following her stepfather's remarriage. In John Mottley's account in his A Compleat List of all the English Dramatic Poets (1747), Centlivre was found crying at the side of a road by Anthony Hammond, who took her to Cambridge University, where she studied under the guise of a young man. Alternatively, the more likely account states that Centlivre joined a company of actors in Stamford, a town 25 miles from her hometown of Holbeach, eventually making her way to the London theatrical scene. has been already related in the sketch of her life affixed to her comedy of the "Busy Body:" the difficulties under which she had to struggle for subsistence, may plead some excuse to the indulgent, for her having in this one production, out of those which now keep a place upon the stage, applied to that disgraceful support of her Muse, to which her own sex of those times did not blush to attend as auditors. Nor can her offence be treated with excessive rigour in reference to the present time by those, who consider, that this very play of "A bold Stroke for a Wife," is now frequently performed to an elegant, yet applauding audience.

The authoress has displayed high dramatic talents in the conception and execution of the various characters and incidents with which this play abounds.
Herein the genius of Mrs. Centlivre consisted—the dialogue of her dramas might be given by a common writer, but her fable and events are proofs of a very extraordinary capacity.

But, in this comedy, however fertile her imagination has been in forming a multiplicity of occurrences, and diversifying the whole exhibition by variety of character, probability is so often violated, that the effect, though powerful, is that of farce, and not genuine comedy.
To admire Mrs. Centlivre as her talents deserve, it is necessary to read, or to see, her "Wonder, or a Woman keeps a Secret."

The following comedy was brought upon the stage in 1717, when the authoress was in her thirty-eighth year.The first performance of this play was staged at the Lincoln's Inn Fields Theatre on February 3rd, 1718, at which point Centlivre was around the age of 49. She enjoyed at that time the intimacy and friendship of Farquhar, Rowe, Steele, and other men of letters, to whom her conversation was highly delightful; as it is said, she had more wit and repartee in herself, than she ever gave to her dramatic characters.

Congreve, who lived in her time, in an exception among the literary men who courted her acquaintance; for he had the humility to be jealous of the favour with which her works were received by the public.Inchbald draws the notion of William Congreve's jealousy of Centlivre from David Erskine Baker's The Companion to the Play-House (1764, volume II, p. unnumbered).

The "Bold Stroke for a Wife" is the drama on which the well known prediction of Wilkes, the celebrated comedian, was delivered, upon his hearing it read previous to its rehearsal. As the first part of that prediction failed,—so is it ardently to be hoped, did the last.Inchbald references the tradition first stated in David Erskine Baker's The Companion to the Play-House in which Robert Wilks remarked "that not only her Play would be damn'd, but she herself be damn'd for writing it" (1764, volume II, p. unnumbered).
